claim: LLMs fundamentally perform next-word prediction: feed in sequence of words, network predicts next word by sampling from probability distribution.
actionable: Understand that all LLM behavior (question-answering, reasoning, instruction-following) emerges from optimizing the next-word prediction objective during training.
source_title: [1hr Talk] Intro to Large Language Models

claim: LLM weights are best understood as lossy compression of the internet: 10TB text compresses ~100x into 140GB parameters, retaining statistical patterns but not exact memorization.
actionable: Expect LLMs to reproduce general patterns and knowledge from training data but to hallucinate unseen details rather than retrieve exact facts; verify outputs against ground truth for factual claims.
source_title: [1hr Talk] Intro to Large Language Models

claim: LLM pre-training (Stage 1) requires ~6,000 GPUs for 12 days at ~$2M cost per 70B model; fine-tuning (Stage 2) is computationally cheaper (~1 day) and can be iterated weekly for alignment.
actionable: Budget roughly $1M+ per month for continuous pre-training iteration, but fine-tuning for alignment/instruction-following is affordable enough to do iteratively each week.
source_title: [1hr Talk] Intro to Large Language Models

claim: Fine-tuning doesn't fix hallucinations or dreams—it only redirects them toward helpful assistant patterns. Always verify LLM outputs from memory alone; trust higher when model has browsed or retrieved relevant context.
actionable: When using LLMs for factual queries, prefer those with access to retrieved documents or web search over pure generative responses; explicitly ask models to cite sources.
source_title: [1hr Talk] Intro to Large Language Models

claim: Scaling laws are highly predictable: accuracy on next-word prediction depends smoothly only on parameter count (N) and training data (D), with no signs of saturation across tested scales.
actionable: Expect measurable capability gains by scaling up model size and/or training data; don't wait for algorithmic breakthroughs—empirically, scale alone drives improvement.
source_title: [1hr Talk] Intro to Large Language Models

claim: Tool use (code execution, web search, image generation, calculators) is essential for LLM reasoning; models learn to emit special tokens requesting tool invocation and consume tool outputs as context.
actionable: Integrate LLMs with external tools (Python/JS executors, search APIs, calculators); train models to output standardized tokens to request tool invocation and process returned results in context window.
source_title: [1hr Talk] Intro to Large Language Models

claim: Knowledge in LLMs is one-directional and lossy: reversing queries fails ('Who is Tom Cruise's mother' works, but 'Who is Mary's son' fails even with correct answer in training).
actionable: Phrase queries from expected angles; if a reversal fails, reframe the question in forward direction or use retrieval-augmented generation to bypass this asymmetry.
source_title: [1hr Talk] Intro to Large Language Models

claim: System 1 vs System 2: current LLMs only have System 1 (fast, instinctive); they cannot spend time reasoning over a tree of possibilities. Future work aims to give LLMs System 2 with extended thinking.
actionable: For complex reasoning, scaffold LLM outputs by asking for step-by-step reasoning; future models with extended thinking tokens may improve this natively.
source_title: [1hr Talk] Intro to Large Language Models

claim: Self-improvement via reinforcement learning (RL) is possible in narrow domains with fast reward functions (e.g., game wins), but lacks clear analogue in open-ended language tasks due to absence of simple reward criterion.
actionable: For domain-specific applications with measurable outcomes (coding bugs fixed, test passes), consider RL fine-tuning; for open-ended tasks, rely on human feedback until domain-specific metrics emerge.
source_title: [1hr Talk] Intro to Large Language Models

claim: Jailbreak attacks bypass safety through encoding (base64, adversarial suffixes, noise patterns) rather than semantic meaning, because safety training typically covers only English harmful requests.
actionable: When implementing safety measures, cover multilingual data and multiple encoding formats (base64, ROT13, etc.); recognize that re-optimizing adversarial suffixes will generate new jailbreaks.
source_title: [1hr Talk] Intro to Large Language Models

claim: Prompt injection attacks hijack LLM behavior by embedding new instructions in retrieved content (web pages, images, documents); attackers can exfiltrate data by crafting instructions into shared docs.
actionable: When LLMs access external documents (files, web), sanitize content; use Content Security Policies; isolate agent-controlled resources; never trust all text in retrieved contexts as benign.
source_title: [1hr Talk] Intro to Large Language Models

claim: Data poisoning via trigger phrases: malicious training data can embed hidden activation patterns that corrupt model behavior when trigger word appears, difficult to patch by blacklisting single examples.
actionable: Curate training data sources carefully; monitor for distribution shifts in fine-tuning datasets; test models with adversarial inputs containing rare tokens to detect poisoning.
source_title: [1hr Talk] Intro to Large Language Models

claim: LLMs are primarily empirical artifacts: we understand the architecture fully, but not how 100B+ parameters collaborate in practice. Interpretability research is advancing but incomplete.
actionable: Approach LLM capabilities as black boxes measured by empirical testing; do not rely on theoretical guarantees about behavior; evaluate thoroughly before deployment.
source_title: [1hr Talk] Intro to Large Language Models
